content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

March 9, 2016

Albert S. Yang
Deputy District Attorney
250 Hamilton Avenue
Palo Alto, CA 94301

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-16-047

Dear Mr. Yang:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of City of Palo Alto Mayor Patrick Burt; Council members Marc Bernan, Eric Filseth, Karen Holman, and Greg Scharff; and Assistant City Manager Suzanne Mason regarding their duties under the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090.

QUESTION

Is it reasonably foreseeable that the City Council's decisions related to a non-profit Transportation Management Association ("TMA") will materially affect the officials' real property interests in or around downtown Palo Alto?

CONCLUSION

It is not reasonably foreseeable that the City Council's decisions related to funding sources for the non-profit TMA will materially affect the officials' real property interests in or around downtown Palo Alto.

FACTS

On March 14, 2016, the Palo Alto City Council will convene a study session regarding the recent formation of a non-profit Transportation Management Association to provide transportation services to private Palo Alto businesses and their employees, focusing initially on business

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-16-047
Page No. 2

located in the City's University Avenue downtown area (the "UAD"). That night, the Council will
also consider an action item regarding local funding strategies for Transportation Demand
Management ("TDM") and related programs.

Among the issues that may be discussed under these agenda items are: (1) whether the City
should adjust the level of financial support for the TMA, from public or private sources; (2) whether
the City should advocate for the TMA to expand its programs; and (3) whether the Council should
initiate a public process to develop new local funding mechanisms (e.g. a tax measure) for the TMA
or other TDM strategies.

The TMA is a non-profit organization created to administer a variety of transportation
demand reduction strategies in Palo Alto, particularly downtown. Initial funding for the TMA is
provided by several downtown businesses as well as the City. The TMA is governed by a six-
person board, on which the City holds one seat. The TMA board has adopted a goal of reducing
single-occupant vehicle trips to downtown Palo Alto by 30%, which the TMA board believes could
reasonably be achieved within 4-5 years. If the TMA is successful, it will likely reduce traffic
downtown as well as the intrusion of commercial parking into the residential neighborhoods
surrounding the UAD. You described the official's interests as follows:

• Council members Berman and Filseth own residential real property within 500 feet of the
UAD and within the boundaries of the downtown residential parking permit ("RPP")
program.

• Council member Holman owns residential real property within 1,000 feet of the UAD and
within the boundaries of the RPP.

• Mayor Burt owns residential real property more than 2,000 feet from the UAD, but within
500 feet of the boundaries of downtown RPP program.

• Council member Scharff owns commercial real property within 500 feet of the UAD and
within the boundaries of the downtown RPP program.

• Assistant City Manager Mason leases residential real property within 500 feet of the UAD
and inside the RDD. Although Mason will not participate in the Council items on March 14,
2016, she will serve as Palo Alto's representative on the TMA board and will have input on
the City's positions with respect to the TMA.

In your March 8, 2016, communication, you clarified that the City Council has no authority
to dissolve the TMA, as the TMA is a separate entity and that defunding or disassociating from the
TMA is not an issue.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest
as set forth in Section 87103. Based upon the facts provided, you have identified the official's

File No. A-16-047
Page No. 3

various real property interests and Councilmember Scharff's business interest. Accordingly, we
must determine whether it is foreseeable that the decisions in question will have a material financial
effect on the properties in and business interest in question. (Section 87103(b).)

Foreseeability

A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
governmental decision on a public official's interests is material. The standard for foreseeability
differs depending on whether an interest is explicitly involved in the decision. An interest is
explicitly involved in a decision if the interest is a named party in, or the subject of, the
governmental decision. (Regulation 18701(a).) Under the facts you present, the interests identified
are not explicitly involved in the decisions before the City. In such cases, the general rule for
foreseeability is: "if the financial effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than
hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable. If the financial result cannot be expected
absent extraordinary circumstances not subject to the public official's control, it is not reasonably
foreseeable."

Note that the determination of foreseeability considers the effects resulting from the actual
decision(s) in question. In your March 8, 2016, communication, you clarified that the City Council
was not considering whether to dissolve the TMA, defund or disassociate from the TMA. Rather,
they would consider: (1) whether the City should adjust the level of financial support for the TMA,
from public or private sources; (2) whether the City should advocate for the TMA to expand its
programs; and (3) whether the Council should initiate a public process to develop new local funding
mechanisms (e.g. a tax measure) for the TMA or other strategies.

Regulation 18701(b) provides several factors that can be considered in reaching the
foreseeability determination. Most pertinent to your inquiry:

(1) The extent to which the occurrence of the financial effect is contingent upon intervening
events, not including future governmental decisions by the official's agency, or any other agency
appointed by or subject to the budgetary control of the official's agency.

You noted that any impact is contingent on the future decisions of the TMA board and that
because the TMA is governed by its own board of directors, on which the City holds only one seat,
the City Council's decisions regarding the TMA are necessarily attenuated from any impact on the
officials' financial interests. In addition, the TMA is expected to experiment with a variety of pilot
programs, including incentivizing lower wage earners to use public transit, none of which have
guaranteed outcomes.

Looking at the decisions in question, all of which involve the refinement of the funding to
the TMA, we agree it would not be a realistic possibility that these decisions would affect property
values or Council member Scharff's business. Moreover, any financial effects of the decision would
not be more than nominal, inconsequential, or insignificant. (Regulation 18702(b).) Of course were

File No. A-16-047
Page No. 4

actual plans or proposals come before the city, we would need to analyze these questions based on
the facts and circumstances involved.²

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Assistant General Counsel
Legal Division

JWW:jgl

________________________
² We note that we reach no conclusion regarding particular decisions by Assistant City Manager Mason while
serving on the TMA Board. If Assistant City Manager Mason needs assistance in determining whether any particular
decision before the board may have a reasonably foreseeable material effect on her interest in her leased residence, she
may wish to seek further advice describing the nature of the decision.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Is it reasonably foreseeable that the City Council's decisions related to a non-profit 
Transportation Management Association ("TMA") will materially affect the officials' real property 
interests in or around downtown Palo Alto?

CONCLUSION: It is not reasonably foreseeable that the City Council's decisions related to funding sources 
for the non-profit TMA will materially affect the officials' real property interests in or around 
downtown Palo Alto.
